reasoning: Candidate tile (outdoor_wall_broken_v00) is a light sandy-beige surface with scattered bright noise pixels — pinks, yellows, random grays, occasional teal flecks. Peer set is unambiguously dark-toned: stone grays, near-blacks, dark earthy browns, with teal as an accent, all rendered in classic hard-stepped pixel art with clear intentional feature placement. (1) Palette overlap: near-zero. Peers live in value range ~20-120/255; candidate base is ~180-200/255. Completely separate color spaces. (2) Pixel density: peers pack rich detail into each 32x32 cell — cracks, mortar lines, rubble, stains. Candidate shows scattered random-looking dots with no cohesive structural features. (3) Shading: peers use hard-stepped value steps. Candidate has no legible shading logic — looks like noise dithering. (4) Warmth/saturation: peers are cool and largely desaturated dungeon aesthetic; candidate is warm sandy beige with saturated bright flecks. Exact opposite bias. (5) Hand-pixeled charm: peers have clear authorial intentionality; candidate reads as procedurally generated noise. (6) Photo-realism: not a primary issue for either, but candidate lacks any pixel art identity. (7) Overall feel: peers are unmistakably a dark fantasy outdoor/dungeon game; candidate could be from a completely different genre (desert exploration, mobile puzzle game). Even granting that a 'broken wall' may differ in surface type from floor/ground peers, the STYLE and PALETTE divergence is categorical, not subtle. This tile would visually rupture any scene it shares with the peer set.
